answer:
SSR vs CSR — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

📘 Definition

SSR (Server-Side Rendering) vs CSR (Client-Side Rendering) represents the fundamental architecture decision for web applications. SSR renders HTML on the server (faster FCP, better SEO), while CSR renders entirely in the browser (faster interactions after initial load, simpler deployment). An interviewer-grade understanding covers: the rendering spectrum (static → SSG → SSR → streaming SSR → ISR → CSR), the trade-off between Time to First Byte (TTFB) and Time to Interactive (TTI), hydration cost (SSR's hidden penalty), and when each approach is optimal based on content dynamicity, SEO requirements, and user interaction patterns.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's Projects

The CRM Dashboard used pure CSR (Angular SPA) — authenticated users behind login, no SEO needed, heavy interactivity (Kanban drag-and-drop, real-time charts). ContactsHub also used CSR (React SPA with Vite) — personal contact manager, no public content. The HR Leave System was CSR too (Angular) — internal enterprise app. If Manoj were building a public-facing product catalog or blog, he'd choose SSR/SSG for SEO and fast FCP. The key decision framework: public content + SEO → SSR/SSG; authenticated interactive dashboard → CSR; mixed (marketing + dashboard) → hybrid with Next.js or Angular Universal.

📊 Rendering Strategy Decision Framework

💻 Code Example — SSR vs CSR Architecture Comparison

// CSR: Vite + React SPA (ContactsHub)
// index.html — empty shell, JS renders everything
// 

// 

// main.tsx — client renders entire app
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client';
createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!).render();

// Performance characteristics:
// FCP: ~1.5s (download JS → parse → render)
// TTI: ~2.0s (same, plus hydration of React tree)
// SEO: None (empty HTML until JS runs)

// ---

// SSR: Next.js App Router (hypothetical product page)
// app/products/[id]/page.tsx
export default async function ProductPage({ params }: { params: { id: string } }) {
  // Runs on server — data fetched before HTML sent
  const product = await prisma.product.findUnique({ where: { id: params.id } });

  return (
    

      
{product.name}

      
{product.description}

       {/* Server-rendered HTML */}
       {/* Client component — hydrates */}
    

  );
}

// Performance characteristics:
// FCP: ~0.3s (HTML arrives with content)
// TTI: ~1.2s (hydration of interactive components)
// SEO: Full (search engines see complete HTML)

// Hybrid: Server component (no JS) + Client component (interactive)
// Only AddToCartButton ships JS to the client
'use client'; // Mark as client component
function AddToCartButton({ productId }: { productId: string }) {
  const [adding, setAdding] = useState(false);
  const handleAdd = async () => {
    setAdding(true);
    await addToCart(productId);
    setAdding(false);
  };
  return ;
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

When the CRM Dashboard team considered SSR for "faster loading," Manoj ran a benchmark comparison. The CSR Angular SPA loaded in 1.8s (FCP) on a good connection — acceptable for authenticated internal users. An SSR prototype with Angular Universal added 400ms TTFB (server rendering time) and 800ms hydration overhead, making TTI actually slower (2.6s vs 2.0s for CSR). The SSR approach also required a Node.js server instead of static CDN hosting, increasing infrastructure cost. The conclusion: CSR was the right choice for the auth-only dashboard. Manoj documented the benchmark results and decision rationale in the team's ADR (Architecture Decision Record) to prevent the discussion from recurring.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
SSR for auth-only appsServer cost + hydration overhead with no SEO benefitUse CSR for authenticated dashboards; SSR only when SEO/FCP matters
CSR for content-heavy public sitesBlank page for search engines; slow FCP on mobileUse SSG/SSR for public content; CSR for interactive portions
Hydration mismatch errorsServer HTML differs from client render; UI flickersEnsure server/client render identical output; use suppressHydrationWarning sparingly
SSR blocking on slow data fetchesTTFB increases with slow DB queriesStreaming SSR to send shell first; Suspense for progressive loading

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Rendering strategy ADR: Documented decision with benchmark data for each project
  • Lighthouse audit: FCP < 1s for SSR pages, < 2s for CSR; TTI < 3s for both
  • SEO test: Google Search Console verification for SSR pages; no indexing issues
  • Hydration error monitoring: Log hydration mismatches in production; target zero
  • Infrastructure cost comparison: CDN-only (CSR) vs Node.js server (SSR) cost tracked quarterly

SSR vs CSR at Scale — Architecture Strategy

📘 Definition

SSR vs CSR at scale requires infrastructure decisions for 1M+ users: SSR needs horizontally-scaled Node.js servers with caching layers (CDN edge caching, in-memory render cache), while CSR needs only CDN-hosted static files but requires client-side performance optimization for diverse devices. At scale, the hybrid approach dominates: SSR for initial page load (cacheable at CDN edge), CSR for subsequent navigation (instant client-side routing), with streaming SSR and progressive hydration reducing the worst-case scenarios of both approaches.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's Scaling Scenario

If ContactsHub scaled to 1M users as a public contact directory (hypothetical), Manoj would implement: SSG for the directory listing (pre-rendered at build, cached at CDN edge, TTFB < 50ms), SSR for individual contact profiles (dynamic data, cacheable for 60s at edge), and CSR for the authenticated management dashboard (after login, full SPA). This hybrid approach would serve 95% of traffic from CDN cache (SSG pages), 4% from edge-cached SSR, and only 1% hitting origin servers — supporting 1M users on a 2-node backend.

📊 Hybrid Rendering at Scale Architecture

💻 Code Example — Hybrid Next.js Architecture

// SSG: Directory listing — built at deploy time
// app/directory/page.tsx
export default async function DirectoryPage() {
  const contacts = await prisma.contact.findMany({
    where: { isPublic: true },
    select: { id: true, name: true, avatar: true, company: true },
    orderBy: { name: 'asc' }
  });

  return (
    

      
Contact Directory

      
    

  );
}

// Static generation — cached at CDN edge forever (until redeploy)
export const revalidate = false; // SSG — never revalidate

// SSR: Individual profile — dynamic, cached at edge 60s
// app/contacts/[id]/page.tsx
export default async function ContactProfile({ params }: { params: { id: string } }) {
  const contact = await prisma.contact.findUnique({
    where: { id: params.id },
    include: { tags: true, notes: true }
  });

  if (!contact) notFound();

  return (
    

      
      
      {/* Client component for interactive features */}
      
    

  );
}

// ISR — revalidate every 60s at edge
export const revalidate = 60;

// CSR: Authenticated dashboard
// app/dashboard/layout.tsx
'use client';
export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) {
  const { user, isLoading } = useAuth();
  if (isLoading) return ;
  if (!user) redirect('/login');
  return {children};
}

// CDN cache headers for optimal caching
// next.config.js
module.exports = {
  async headers() {
    return [
      { source: '/directory', headers: [{ key: 'Cache-Control', value: 's-maxage=86400' }] },
      { source: '/contacts/:id', headers: [{ key: 'Cache-Control', value: 's-maxage=60, stale-while-revalidate=300' }] },
      { source: '/dashboard/:path*', headers: [{ key: 'Cache-Control', value: 'private, no-cache' }] },
    ];
  }
};

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

During a scale planning exercise, Manoj calculated infrastructure costs for different rendering strategies at 1M monthly users. Pure SSR: every request hits a Node.js server → 10 instances needed → $500/month. Hybrid (SSG + ISR + CSR): 95% served from CDN → 2 instances for cache misses → $50/month. The 10x cost difference made the hybrid approach the clear winner. He validated with load testing: Vegeta sent 1,000 RPS to the SSG directory page — CDN served all requests at P99 < 20ms, never reaching origin. The ISR contact profiles had P99 < 100ms (60s cache hit rate: 98%). Only the authenticated dashboard hit origin servers.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
SSR without caching at scaleEvery request renders on server; O(n) server costCDN edge caching with s-maxage; stale-while-revalidate
SSG with too many pagesBuild time grows linearly with page countOn-demand ISR for long-tail pages; SSG only for top 1000
Cache invalidation complexityStale data shown to users after updatesWebhooks for targeted cache purge; revalidatePath() in Next.js
CDN cache poisoningUser-specific data cached and served to wrong usersNever cache authenticated responses at CDN; use private for auth pages

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Cache hit rate: CDN analytics showing > 95% hit rate for SSG, > 90% for ISR pages
  • Load test: 1,000 RPS to SSG page → P99 < 50ms at CDN edge
  • Cost monitoring: Monthly infrastructure cost vs. user count; target < $0.001/user
  • Cache freshness: ISR revalidation time < 60s; verified with timestamp in response
  • Auth leak test: Verify authenticated dashboard pages have Cache-Control: private

Hydration — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

Hydration performance bottlenecks occur when the browser must execute JavaScript to make server-rendered HTML interactive. The hydration process re-executes component logic, attaches event listeners, and reconciles the server HTML with the client's virtual DOM. Bottlenecks include: full-page hydration blocking TTI (Time to Interactive), hydration mismatch errors causing re-renders, large JavaScript payloads delaying hydration start, and "uncanny valley" — the period where content is visible but not interactive. Instrumentation measures hydration duration, JS payload size, and the gap between FCP and TTI.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's Scaling Scenario

If ContactsHub's public directory used SSR, the contact list page would render 50 contacts as HTML on the server (FCP: 300ms). But hydration would require downloading 180 KB of JS and executing it to attach click handlers, search functionality, and infinite scroll — adding 1.2s before the page was interactive. Manoj would implement selective hydration: the contact list uses server components (zero client JS), only the search bar and action buttons hydrate as client components. This reduces the hydration JS from 180 KB to 25 KB, cutting TTI from 1.5s to 500ms.

📊 Hydration Timeline Analysis

💻 Code Example — Selective Hydration Strategy

// Server Component: zero client JS — no hydration needed
// app/directory/page.tsx (default: server component)
export default async function DirectoryPage() {
  const contacts = await prisma.contact.findMany({
    where: { isPublic: true },
    take: 50,
    select: { id: true, name: true, email: true, avatar: true }
  });

  return (
    

      
Contact Directory

      {/* Client component: only this part hydrates (25 KB) */}
      }>
          {/* 'use client' — hydrates for interactivity */}
      

      {/* Server component: pure HTML, zero JS shipped */}
      

        {contacts.map(contact => (
          
        ))}
      


      {/* Client component: hydrates for infinite scroll */}
      }>
         {/* 'use client' — tiny hydration */}
      
    

  );
}

// ContactCard: server component — NO hydration, NO JS
function ContactCard({ contact }: { contact: Contact }) {
  return (
    

      
      
{contact.name}

      {contact.email}
      {/* Links work without JS — progressive enhancement */}
      View Profile →
    

  );
}

// SearchBar: client component — hydrates for real-time search
'use client';
function SearchBar() {
  const [search, setSearch] = useState('');
  const router = useRouter();
  // Only this 25KB component needs JS
  return (
     setSearch(e.target.value)}
      onKeyDown={e => e.key === 'Enter' && router.push(`/directory?q=${search}`)}
      placeholder="Search contacts..."
    />
  );
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In a POC for a public ContactsHub directory, Lighthouse showed FCP at 300ms (great) but TTI at 1.5s (poor) — a 1.2s "uncanny valley" where users could see contacts but couldn't interact. The JavaScript bundle analyzer showed 180 KB of React + component code being hydrated for the entire page, even though 90% of the content (contact cards) was static HTML. Manoj refactored contact cards to server components (zero JS) and only hydrated the search bar and infinite scroll trigger. The JS payload dropped from 180 KB to 25 KB, and TTI improved to 500ms. He added a custom Web Vitals metric tracking the FCP-TTI gap, alerting if it exceeded 500ms.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Full-page hydration for mostly-static contentUnnecessary JS download and executionServer Components for static content; client components only for interactive parts
Hydration mismatch (server ≠ client HTML)React re-renders from scratch; FCP advantage lostAvoid Date.now(), Math.random() in render; use consistent data sources
Large third-party libraries in client componentsHydration blocked on library download/parseDynamic import heavy libraries; use next/dynamic with SSR disabled for charts
CSS-in-JS flash during hydrationStyled components re-inject styles client-side; flash of unstyled contentUse CSS Modules or Tailwind (static CSS); or server-extract critical CSS

✅ Validation Strategy

  • FCP-TTI gap: Custom Web Vital; alert if gap > 500ms
  • Hydration JS size: Bundle analyzer; client component JS < 50 KB per page
  • Hydration mismatch monitor: React's onRecoverableError logged to error tracking; target zero
  • Lighthouse CI: TTI < 1s on simulated 4G mobile
  • Progressive enhancement test: Disable JS → verify all links and content still accessible

Hydration — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

📘 Definition

Hydration is the process of making server-rendered HTML interactive by attaching JavaScript event handlers and reconciling with the client-side virtual DOM. An interviewer-grade understanding covers: full hydration vs. progressive hydration vs. partial hydration (islands architecture), React 18's streaming SSR with Suspense-based selective hydration, the reconciliation process during hydration (React walks the DOM tree matching it to the component tree), resumability (Qwik's approach of serializing event handlers to avoid hydration entirely), and the metrics that matter (TTI, FCP-TTI gap, Total Blocking Time).

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub Architecture

ContactsHub's PWA offline-first architecture highlighted a unique hydration challenge: service worker-cached HTML needed to hydrate identically to server-rendered HTML. If the service worker served stale HTML while the client bundle was updated (new deploy), hydration mismatches caused full client-side re-renders, destroying the PWA's instant-load advantage. Manoj implemented versioned asset hashing: the HTML, service worker, and JS bundle all referenced the same build hash, ensuring hydration consistency. The service worker pre-cached the matching JS bundle during install, eliminating version mismatches.

📊 Hydration Approaches Comparison

💻 Code Example — React 18 Selective Hydration

// React 18 streaming SSR with selective hydration
// server.tsx
import { renderToPipeableStream } from 'react-dom/server';

app.get('*', (req, res) => {
  const stream = renderToPipeableStream(, {
    bootstrapScripts: ['/main.js'],
    onShellReady() {
      // Send HTML shell immediately — FCP is fast
      res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/html');
      stream.pipe(res);
    },
    onError(err) {
      console.error('SSR error:', err);
    }
  });
});

// Client: selective hydration with Suspense
// app.tsx
function App() {
  return (
    
      
        
  {/* Hydrates immediately — navigation must be interactive */}
        }>
           {/* Hydrates second — important for UX */}
        
        }>
           {/* Hydrates last — content already visible as HTML */}
        
        }>
          
 {/* Hydrates last — below fold */}
        
      
    
  );
}

// If user clicks SearchBar before it hydrates,
// React PRIORITIZES hydrating SearchBar immediately!
// This is React 18's selective hydration in action.

// PWA: Versioned hydration consistency
// service-worker.ts
const BUILD_HASH = '__BUILD_HASH__'; // Injected at build time
const CACHE_NAME = `app-${BUILD_HASH}`;

self.addEventListener('install', (event) => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.open(CACHE_NAME).then(cache =>
      cache.addAll([
        '/',
        `/main.${BUILD_HASH}.js`,  // Same hash as HTML references
        `/styles.${BUILD_HASH}.css`
      ])
    )
  );
});

// Clean up old caches to prevent version mismatches
self.addEventListener('activate', (event) => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.keys().then(keys =>
      Promise.all(keys.filter(k => k !== CACHE_NAME).map(k => caches.delete(k)))
    )
  );
});

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

ContactsHub's PWA had an intermittent bug: after a new deploy, some users saw a white flash followed by the content re-rendering. The cause: the service worker served cached HTML from the previous build, but the browser fetched the new JS bundle (different deploy). React's hydration detected a mismatch (component tree structure changed) and fell back to full client-side rendering — erasing the cached HTML's instant FCP advantage. The fix: versioned cache names using build hashes, and the service worker's activate event cleaning up old caches. Post-fix, hydration mismatches dropped from ~200/day to zero. Manoj also added React's onRecoverableError callback to log hydration mismatches to the error tracker.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Browser extensions modifying DOM before hydrationReact detects mismatch; falls back to client renderUse suppressHydrationWarning on body; hydrate on a wrapper div
Service worker serving stale HTML with new JSHydration mismatch; full client re-renderVersioned cache names; clean up old caches on activate
Third-party scripts injecting DOM nodesHydration tree mismatchLoad third-party scripts after hydration via useEffect
Streaming SSR error recoveryPartial HTML sent before error discoveredError boundary in streaming; fallback to client-rendered error page

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Hydration mismatch monitor: onRecoverableError callback in hydrateRoot; target zero mismatches
  • Service worker version check: Startup script comparing HTML build hash with JS bundle hash
  • FCP-TTI gap: Web Vitals measurement; gap < 500ms on 4G mobile
  • Selective hydration test: Click unhydrated component → verify it becomes interactive within 100ms
  • PWA offline test: Playwright offline mode → verify cached HTML hydrates correctly

Accessibility — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

📘 Definition

Web Accessibility (a11y) ensures applications are usable by people with disabilities, including visual, motor, cognitive, and auditory impairments. An interviewer-grade understanding covers: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance (the legal standard in most jurisdictions), ARIA roles/states/properties, keyboard navigation patterns (focus management, tab order, focus trapping in modals), screen reader semantics (live regions, landmark roles), color contrast requirements (4.5:1 for text), and the testing pyramid (automated axe-core → manual keyboard testing → screen reader testing → user testing).

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR Leave System served 1,200+ employees including several with visual impairments who used screen readers (JAWS, NVDA). The initial Angular Material-based UI scored 62/100 on Lighthouse accessibility. Key issues: the 3-level approval chain status wasn't announced to screen readers (missing ARIA live region), custom dropdown menus trapped keyboard focus, and error messages for form validation weren't associated with their fields (aria-describedby missing). Manoj implemented: aria-live="polite" for approval status updates, focus management in custom dropdowns, aria-describedby on all form fields, and skip navigation links. The Lighthouse score improved to 98/100, and screen reader users reported a "night and day" improvement.

📊 Accessibility Testing Pyramid

💻 Code Example — Accessible Angular Components

// Accessible leave approval status with ARIA live region
@Component({
  selector: 'app-approval-status',
  template: `
    

      
      @switch (leave.status) {
        @case ('pending') {
          
            Pending approval from {{ leave.currentApprover }}
          
        }
        @case ('approved') {
          
            Approved by {{ leave.approvedBy }} on {{ leave.approvedDate | date }}
          
        }
        @case ('rejected') {
          
            Rejected: {{ leave.rejectionReason }}
          
        }
      }
    

  `,
  changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush
})
export class ApprovalStatusComponent {
  @Input({ required: true }) leave!: LeaveRequest;
}

// Accessible form with error association
@Component({
  selector: 'app-leave-form',
  template: `
    

      

        
        
        @if (startDate.invalid && startDate.touched) {
          

            {{ getErrorMessage('startDate') }}
          

        }
      


      
      Skip to main content

      
      

        
Confirm Leave Request

        
Submit {{ dayCount }} days of {{ leaveType }} leave?

        
        
      

    

  `
})
export class LeaveFormComponent {
  // Focus management: return focus to trigger after dialog closes
  private triggerElement: HTMLElement | null = null;

  openConfirmDialog(event: Event) {
    this.triggerElement = event.target as HTMLElement;
    this.showConfirmDialog = true;
  }

  cancel() {
    this.showConfirmDialog = false;
    this.triggerElement?.focus(); // Return focus to trigger
  }
}

// axe-core in Playwright E2E tests
import AxeBuilder from '@axe-core/playwright';

test('leave form is accessible', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('/leaves/new');
  const results = await new AxeBuilder({ page })
    .withTags(['wcag2a', 'wcag2aa'])
    .analyze();
  expect(results.violations).toEqual([]);
});

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

An HR employee who used NVDA (screen reader) reported that leave approval status changes weren't announced — they had to manually navigate to the status section to check updates. Manoj tested with NVDA and confirmed: the status badge used a plain <span> with no ARIA attributes. When Socket.io pushed a status update and Angular re-rendered the badge, NVDA was silent. Adding role="status" and aria-live="polite" made NVDA announce status changes automatically. He also discovered that the custom date picker was completely inaccessible — no keyboard support, no ARIA roles. He replaced it with Angular Material's datepicker which had full a11y support, then added axe-core to every Playwright test to prevent future regressions.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Custom components without ARIA rolesScreen readers can't interpret purposeUse native HTML elements when possible; add ARIA roles to custom widgets
Form errors not associated with fieldsScreen readers don't announce which field has the erroraria-describedby linking field to error message; role="alert" on errors
Focus not managed in modals/dialogsKeyboard users trapped or lost after dialog interactionscdkTrapFocus (Angular) or focus-trap library; return focus on close
Color-only status indicatorsColor-blind users can't distinguish statesAlways pair color with text/icon; ensure 4.5:1 contrast ratio

✅ Validation Strategy

  • axe-core in CI: Every Playwright test includes AxeBuilder check; zero violations required
  • Lighthouse a11y: Score ≥ 95 in CI; block deployment on regression
  • Keyboard-only navigation test: Quarterly manual test of all workflows using only keyboard
  • Screen reader testing: Monthly testing with NVDA (Windows) and VoiceOver (Mac)
  • Color contrast audit: All text and interactive elements meet 4.5:1 AA contrast ratio

Accessibility at Scale — Architecture Strategy

📘 Definition

Accessibility at scale means embedding a11y into the development workflow so that every feature, component, and page meets WCAG 2.1 AA standards by default. At 1M+ users, this requires: an accessible component library (design system with built-in a11y), automated testing in CI/CD, a11y-first design process, and monitoring for regressions. The goal is shifting a11y left — catching issues at component design time rather than in production audits.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub used shadcn/ui components (built on Radix UI primitives with built-in a11y). This provided keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA attributes out of the box for dialogs, dropdowns, and tooltips. Manoj extended the Playwright test suite (60+ E2E tests) with axe-core checks on every page transition. When a developer added a custom contact import dialog without focus trapping, the axe-core test caught it immediately: dialog-focus-trap violation. The fix took 5 minutes (adding Radix's Dialog.Portal with auto-focus). This shift-left approach caught 15 a11y issues in the first month, all before they reached production.

📊 Accessibility-First Development Pipeline

💻 Code Example — Accessible Component Library + Testing

// shadcn/ui Dialog with built-in a11y (Radix primitives)
import {
  Dialog, DialogTrigger, DialogContent,
  DialogHeader, DialogTitle, DialogDescription
} from '@/components/ui/dialog';

function ImportContactsDialog() {
  return (
    
      
        
      
      {/* Radix handles: focus trap, Escape to close, aria-modal,
          aria-labelledby, return focus to trigger on close */}
      
        
          Import Contacts
          
            Upload a CSV file to bulk import contacts.
          
        
        
        
      
    
  );
}

// ESLint jsx-a11y configuration
// .eslintrc
{
  "extends": ["plugin:jsx-a11y/strict"],
  "rules": {
    "jsx-a11y/no-autofocus": "error",
    "jsx-a11y/anchor-is-valid": "error",
    "jsx-a11y/click-events-have-key-events": "error",
    "jsx-a11y/no-noninteractive-element-interactions": "error"
  }
}

// Playwright + axe-core helper
import AxeBuilder from '@axe-core/playwright';

// Shared helper for all E2E tests
async function checkA11y(page: Page, testInfo: TestInfo) {
  const results = await new AxeBuilder({ page })
    .withTags(['wcag2a', 'wcag2aa', 'wcag21aa'])
    .analyze();

  // Attach violations to test report for debugging
  if (results.violations.length > 0) {
    await testInfo.attach('a11y-violations', {
      body: JSON.stringify(results.violations, null, 2),
      contentType: 'application/json'
    });
  }

  expect(results.violations).toEqual([]);
}

// Usage in every test
test('import dialog is accessible', async ({ page }, testInfo) => {
  await page.goto('/contacts');
  await page.click('button:has-text("Import Contacts")');
  await checkA11y(page, testInfo);
});

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After adding a custom tag-selection combobox to ContactsHub, a Playwright axe-core test failed with: aria-required-children: Required ARIA child role not present. The custom combobox used role="listbox" but the option elements were plain <div>s without role="option". Manoj replaced the custom implementation with Radix UI's Combobox primitive which handled all ARIA roles correctly. He also enabled Storybook's a11y addon, which showed live violation alerts while developing components — catching issues before they even reached CI. The team established a rule: all interactive components must use Radix primitives or be manually audited for keyboard + screen reader support.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Custom interactive components without ARIAInaccessible to screen readers and keyboard usersUse Radix/Headless UI primitives; never build custom dropdowns from scratch
axe-core only catches ~30% of a11y issuesAutomated tests give false sense of complianceLayer: axe-core + keyboard manual test + quarterly screen reader audit
Accessibility regression after UI changesPreviously accessible features breakaxe-core in every Playwright test; Lighthouse a11y in CI
Dynamic content not announced to screen readersStatus changes, toasts invisible to SR usersaria-live regions for dynamic content; test with NVDA/VoiceOver

✅ Validation Strategy

  • CI gate: axe-core in every Playwright test; zero WCAG 2.1 AA violations
  • Lighthouse a11y: Score ≥ 95; block deployment on regression
  • Storybook a11y addon: Live violations visible during component development
  • ESLint jsx-a11y: Strict rules catching issues in editor before commit
  • Quarterly audit: Manual keyboard + screen reader testing of all critical workflows
